title: Generate subsets
description: Generate all subsets of an array using recursion

examples.explanation: n = 2, total_subsets = 4. Mask 0 (00): bits 0 and 1 are 0 → subset = []. Mask 1 (01): bit 0=1 → include nums[0]=1, bit 1=0 → subset = [1]. Mask 2 (10): bit 0=0, bit 1=1 → include nums[1]=2 → subset = [2]. Mask 3 (11): bits 0 and 1 are 1 → include nums[0]=1 and nums[1]=2 → subset = [1, 2]. Result: [[], [1], [2], [1, 2]].
n = 1, total_subsets = 2. Mask 0 (0): bit 0=0 → subset = []. Mask 1 (1): bit 0=1 → include nums[0]=3 → subset = [3]. Result: [[], [3]].

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Iterative Bitmask
approaches.summary: Generate all subsets by iterating through all possible bitmasks from 0 to 2^n - 1, where each bit represents whether to include an element. Use this when you need a simple, non-recursive method to list all subsets.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n * 2^n) for storing all subsets, or O(n) if output is not counted

ladders.title: Full Problem — Generate All Subsets Using Bitmask Iteration
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to generate all subsets of an array using bitmask iteration.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that every subset of an n-element array can be uniquely represented by an n-bit binary number (bitmask), where each bit corresponds to whether an element is included (1) or excluded (0). By iterating through all numbers from 0 to 2^n - 1, we generate all possible bitmasks, and for each mask, we construct the corresponding subset by checking which bits are set. This works because there are exactly 2^n subsets for n elements, matching the range of n-bit numbers. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Determine n = length of input array. 2) Calculate total_subsets = 2^n. 3) For mask from 0 to total_subsets-1: a) Initialize empty subset. b) For i from 0 to n-1: if mask & (1 << i) is non-zero, include array[i] in subset. c) Add subset to result. 4) Return result. Time complexity is O(n * 2^n) because for each of 2^n masks, we iterate through n bits to build subsets. Space complexity is O(n * 2^n) to store all subsets in output, or O(n) if output isn't counted, as we only need temporary storage for one subset at a time. This connects to L1 by using the bitmask-to-subset conversion as the key subroutine. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in loop ranges (should be 0 to 2^n-1 inclusive), incorrect bit checking (using wrong shift direction), and not handling empty input (should return [[]]). The real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def subsets(nums): n = len(nums); result = []; for mask in range(1 << n): subset = []; for i in range(n): if mask & (1 << i): subset.append(nums[i]); result.append(subset); return result.

examples.trace: n = 2, total_subsets = 4. Mask 0 (00): bits 0 and 1 are 0 → subset = []. Mask 1 (01): bit 0=1 → include nums[0]=1, bit 1=0 → subset = [1]. Mask 2 (10): bit 0=0, bit 1=1 → include nums[1]=2 → subset = [2]. Mask 3 (11): bits 0 and 1 are 1 → include nums[0]=1 and nums[1]=2 → subset = [1, 2]. Result: [[], [1], [2], [1, 2]].
n = 1, total_subsets = 2. Mask 0 (0): bit 0=0 → subset = []. Mask 1 (1): bit 0=1 → include nums[0]=3 → subset = [3]. Result: [[], [3]].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Convert Bitmask to Subset
ladders.desc: Given a bitmask and array, extract elements where corresponding bits are set.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that a bitmask encodes inclusion decisions: each bit position i corresponds to array element i, with 1 meaning include and 0 meaning exclude. By checking each bit, we can reconstruct the subset. This works because binary representation provides a direct mapping between bit patterns and subset combinations. Step-by-step: 1) Input: array nums of length n, integer mask. 2) Initialize empty subset list. 3) For i from 0 to n-1: a) Check if the i-th bit of mask is set using bitwise AND: if mask & (1 << i) != 0. b) If set, append nums[i] to subset. 4) Return subset. Time complexity is O(n) as we check n bits. Space complexity is O(n) for the subset list. This feeds into L0 by being the core operation performed for each mask in the full problem. Common mistakes: using right shift instead of left shift (1 << i is correct), forgetting to handle i starting from 0, and not initializing the subset list. Real code pattern: def mask_to_subset(nums, mask): subset = []; for i in range(len(nums)): if mask & (1 << i): subset.append(nums[i]); return subset.

examples.trace: n=3. i=0: mask=5, 1<<0=1, 5&1=1 (non-zero) → include nums[0]=1. i=1: 1<<1=2, 5&2=0 → exclude nums[1]=2. i=2: 1<<2=4, 5&4=4 (non-zero) → include nums[2]=3. Subset = [1, 3].
n=3. i=0: mask=2, 1<<0=1, 2&1=0 → exclude a. i=1: 1<<1=2, 2&2=2 (non-zero) → include b. i=2: 1<<2=4, 2&4=0 → exclude c. Subset = [b].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterate Through All Bitmasks
ladders.desc: Generate all n-bit binary numbers from 0 to 2^n - 1.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to enumerate all subsets, we need to consider every possible combination of including or excluding n elements, which corresponds to all n-bit binary numbers. By counting from 0 to 2^n - 1, we systematically cover all possibilities because an n-bit number can represent 2^n unique values. This works because binary counting naturally progresses through all bit patterns. Step-by-step: 1) Input: integer n (number of elements). 2) Calculate total = 2^n using bit shift: 1 << n. 3) For mask in range(0, total): process mask. Time complexity is O(2^n) for the iteration. Space complexity is O(1) if only counting. This connects to L1 by providing the masks that are converted to subsets. Common mistakes: using range(0, 2^n) which excludes 2^n-1 (should be range(0, 1<<n)), or miscalculating 2^n (use bit shift for efficiency). Real code pattern: for mask in range(1 << n): # process mask.

examples.trace: total = 1 << 2 = 4. Loop mask from 0 to 3 inclusive: mask=0 (binary 00), mask=1 (01), mask=2 (10), mask=3 (11).

ladders.title: Building Block — Check if a Specific Bit is Set
ladders.desc: Determine whether the i-th bit of an integer is 1 using bitwise operations.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that in binary, each bit represents a power of two. To check the i-th bit (0-indexed from right), we can isolate it by creating a mask with only that bit set and using bitwise AND. This works because AND returns 1 only if both bits are 1. Step-by-step: 1) Input: integer num, bit position i. 2) Create bitmask = 1 << i (shifts 1 left by i positions). 3) Perform num & bitmask. 4) If result is non-zero, the bit is set (1); otherwise, it's 0. Time and space complexity are O(1). This feeds into L2 by being used to extract elements for subset construction. Common mistakes: using right shift incorrectly (num >> i checks value, not bit), or off-by-one in indexing (i should start at 0 for least significant bit). Real code pattern: if num & (1 << i): # bit is set.

examples.trace: bitmask = 1 << 0 = 1 (binary 001). 5 & 1 = 1 (non-zero) → bit 0 is set.
bitmask = 1 << 1 = 2 (binary 010). 6 & 2 = 2 (non-zero) → bit 1 is set.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Bitwise Left Shift
ladders.desc: Shift bits of a number left by a specified number of positions.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that left shifting multiplies a number by 2 for each shift, effectively moving bits to higher positions in binary representation. This works because in base-2, shifting left adds zeros to the right, increasing the value by powers of two. Step-by-step: 1) Input: integer x, shift amount k. 2) Compute result = x << k. 3) In binary, bits of x are moved k positions left; new bits on right are 0. For example, 1 << 2 shifts binary 1 (001) left by 2 to become 4 (100). Time and space complexity are O(1). This connects to L3 by creating masks to check specific bits (e.g., 1 << i). Common mistakes: confusing left and right shift, or shifting by negative amounts. Real code pattern: mask = 1 << i.

examples.trace: 1 in binary: 001. Shift left by 3: 001 becomes 1000 (binary) = 8 decimal.
3 in binary: 011. Shift left by 2: 011 becomes 1100 (binary) = 12 decimal.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Number System
ladders.desc: Understand how numbers are represented in base-2 using bits.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that binary (base-2) uses only two digits, 0 and 1, called bits, where each position represents a power of two. This works because it's a positional numeral system: from right to left, positions are 2^0, 2^1, 2^2, etc. For example, binary 101 means 1*2^2 + 0*2^1 + 1*2^0 = 4 + 0 + 1 = 5. Step-by-step to convert decimal to binary: repeatedly divide by 2 and record remainders. For n bits, we can represent numbers from 0 to 2^n - 1. Time and space for understanding are O(1) per number. This feeds into L4 as left shifting operates on binary representations. Common mistakes: confusing bit positions (starting from 0 on right), or misinterpreting binary values. Real code pattern: in programming, integers are stored in binary internally.

examples.trace: 3 divided by 2: quotient 1, remainder 1. 1 divided by 2: quotient 0, remainder 1. Read remainders backward: 11 binary. Check: 1*2^1 + 1*2^0 = 2 + 1 = 3.

6 divided by 2: quotient 3, remainder 0. 3 divided by 2: quotient 1, remainder 1. 1 divided by 2: quotient 0, remainder 1. Remainders: 0, 1, 1 backward → 110 binary. Check: 1*2^2 + 1*2^1 + 0*2^0 = 4 + 2 + 0 = 6.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Backtracking Recursive Inclusion-Exclusion
approaches.summary: Recursively build subsets by making two choices at each element: include it or exclude it, backtracking to explore all possibilities. This is the classic backtracking method for subset generation.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for recursion stack, plus O(n * 2^n) for output if stored

ladders.title: Full Problem — Generate All Subsets via Backtracking Recursive Inclusion-Exclusion
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to generate all subsets of an array using backtracking with include/exclude decisions.
ladders.explanation: This approach systematically explores all possible subsets by making a binary decision at each element: include it in the current subset or exclude it. The intuition is that every subset corresponds to a unique combination of include/exclude choices across all elements. Starting with an empty subset, we recursively process each element, branching to include it (add to current subset) or exclude it (skip). When we reach the end of the array, we record the current subset as one valid result. This backtracking ensures we explore all 2^n possibilities without duplication. The algorithm uses depth-first recursion: at index i, we first explore the branch where we include nums[i], then backtrack by removing it, and explore the exclude branch. The recursion depth is O(n) for the stack, and we generate O(2^n) subsets, each taking O(n) time to copy/store, leading to O(n * 2^n) total time. Space is O(n) for recursion plus O(n * 2^n) for output storage. Connection to L1: This full solution integrates the recursive helper from L1 with initialization and result handling. Common mistakes include forgetting to backtrack (remove element after include branch), not handling base case correctly, or modifying the input array. Real code pattern: def subsets(nums): res = []; def backtrack(i, curr): if i == len(nums): res.append(curr.copy()); return; curr.append(nums[i]); backtrack(i+1, curr); curr.pop(); backtrack(i+1, curr); backtrack(0, []); return res.

examples.trace: Start: res = [], backtrack(0, []). i=0, curr=[]. Include 1: curr=[1], backtrack(1, [1]). i=1, curr=[1]. Include 2: curr=[1,2], backtrack(2, [1,2]). i=2, base case: res=[[1,2]]. Backtrack: pop 2, curr=[1]. Exclude 2: backtrack(2, [1]). i=2, base case: res=[[1,2], [1]]. Backtrack: pop 1, curr=[]. Exclude 1: backtrack(1, []). i=1, curr=[]. Include 2: curr=[2], backtrack(2, [2]). i=2, base case: res=[[1,2], [1], [2]]. Backtrack: pop 2, curr=[]. Exclude 2: backtrack(2, []). i=2, base case: res=[[1,2], [1], [2], []]. Done.

Start: res = [], backtrack(0, []). i=0, curr=[]. Include 3: curr=[3], backtrack(1, [3]). i=1, base case: res=[[3]]. Backtrack: pop 3, curr=[]. Exclude 3: backtrack(1, []). i=1, base case: res=[[3], []]. Done.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Backtracking Helper Function
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that explores include/exclude choices for subset generation.
ladders.explanation: This helper function is the core engine of the subset generation algorithm. The intuition is to recursively traverse the array, at each step deciding whether to include the current element in the subset being built. It takes two parameters: the current index in the array and the current subset (list) being constructed. The base case occurs when the index equals the array length, meaning we've processed all elements, so we record the current subset. Otherwise, we first explore the include branch: add the current element to the subset, recurse to the next index, then backtrack by removing the element. Then we explore the exclude branch: recurse to the next index without adding the element. This ensures all combinations are explored. Time complexity for this helper is O(2^n) calls, each O(1) except base case O(n) to copy, but overall dominated by full problem. Space is O(n) recursion depth. Connection to L0: This helper is called within the full solution to generate subsets. Connection to L2: It implements the core logic of binary decision-making. Common mistakes include not copying the subset before adding to results (leading to references), or incorrect backtracking. Real code pattern: def backtrack(i, curr): if i == n: res.append(curr.copy()); return; curr.append(nums[i]); backtrack(i+1, curr); curr.pop(); backtrack(i+1, curr).

examples.trace: Call backtrack(0, []). i=0, not base. Include: curr=[1], call backtrack(1, [1]). i=1, not base. Include: curr=[1,2], call backtrack(2, [1,2]). i=2, base: record [1,2]. Return, pop 2, curr=[1]. Exclude: call backtrack(2, [1]). i=2, base: record [1]. Return, pop 1, curr=[]. Exclude: call backtrack(1, []). i=1, not base. Include: curr=[2], call backtrack(2, [2]). i=2, base: record [2]. Return, pop 2, curr=[]. Exclude: call backtrack(2, []). i=2, base: record []. Done.
Call backtrack(0, []). i=0, not base. Include: curr=[3], call backtrack(1, [3]). i=1, base: record [3]. Return, pop 3, curr=[]. Exclude: call backtrack(1, []). i=1, base: record []. Done.

testCases.expected: From this state, generate subsets [1] and [1,2]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Binary Decision-Making at Each Element
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the include/exclude choice for each element in subset construction.
ladders.explanation: The central insight is that generating all subsets reduces to making a binary decision (include or exclude) for each element in the array. For an array of size n, there are 2^n possible subsets because each element can either be in or out. This logic is applied sequentially: start with an empty subset, for the first element, choose to include it (forming subset with it) or exclude it (keeping subset empty). For each resulting state, repeat for the next element, branching further. This creates a binary tree of decisions where each leaf is a unique subset. The technique involves recursively exploring both choices: when including, add element to current subset and proceed; when excluding, proceed without adding. After exploring include, we must backtrack by removing the element to restore state before exploring exclude. This ensures all paths are covered without duplication. Time per decision is O(1), leading to O(2^n) total decisions. Space is O(n) for recursion depth. Connection to L1: This logic is embedded in the recursive helper. Connection to L3: It relies on backtracking to manage state. Common mistakes include not backtracking properly or confusing order of include/exclude. Real code pattern: At each index i: include: curr.append(nums[i]); recurse(i+1); curr.pop(); exclude: recurse(i+1).

examples.trace: For element 1 at index 0: Option 1 (include): Add 1 to [], current becomes [1]. Move to next element. Option 2 (exclude): Keep [] as is. Move to next element. After exploring include, remove 1 to backtrack to [] before exploring exclude.

For element 2 at index 1, starting with subset [1]: Option 1 (include): Add 2 to [1], current becomes [1,2]. Reach end, record. Option 2 (exclude): Keep [1] as is. Reach end, record. After include, remove 2 to backtrack to [1].

ladders.title: Building Block — Backtracking with State Management
ladders.desc: Learn to use backtracking to explore choices while maintaining and restoring state.
ladders.explanation: Backtracking is a technique for exploring all possibilities by making a series of choices, and undoing (backtracking) choices to try alternatives. In subset generation, the state is the current subset being built. When we include an element, we modify the state by adding it. After exploring all possibilities from that included state, we must restore the state to what it was before the inclusion, so we can explore the exclude branch from the original state. This is done by removing the element (e.g., using pop() on a list). The intuition is that backtracking allows us to reuse the same state variable efficiently without creating copies at each step, saving space. The algorithm follows a depth-first traversal: go deep into one choice (include), then backtrack, then explore the other choice (exclude). Time complexity involves O(2^n) state modifications, each O(1). Space is O(n) for the recursion stack and state. Connection to L2: This enables the binary decision-making by managing state changes. Connection to L4: It relies on basic list operations for state updates. Common mistakes include forgetting to backtrack (leading to incorrect subsets) or modifying state in place without restoring. Real code pattern: def explore(state): make choice; update state; recurse; undo choice; make other choice; recurse.

examples.trace: Start state: []. Make choice to include 1: update state to [1]. Recurse to explore further (e.g., with next element). After recursion returns, undo choice: remove 1 from state, restoring to []. Now state is ready for exclude choice.
Start state: [1]. Include 2: update to [1,2]. Recurse (reach end, record subset). Undo: remove 2, state back to [1]. Now explore exclude branch from [1].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — List Manipulation for Subset Building
ladders.desc: Perform fundamental operations to add and remove elements from a list representing a subset.
ladders.explanation: In subset generation, we represent a subset as a list (or array). The key operations are adding an element to the list (to include it) and removing the last element (to backtrack after inclusion). Adding is typically done with an append operation, which adds the element to the end of the list in O(1) time. Removing is done with a pop operation, which removes the last element in O(1) time, effectively undoing the last addition. This allows us to build subsets incrementally and revert changes efficiently. The intuition is that by using a single list and modifying it in place, we avoid creating new lists at each step, which would be costly in time and space. We must ensure that pop is called after exploring the include branch to restore the list to its previous state before exploring the exclude branch. Time per add/remove is O(1). Space for the list is O(n) in the worst case. Connection to L3: These operations are used in backtracking for state management. Connection to L5: They rely on understanding lists and indices. Common mistakes include using incorrect indices for pop, or not copying the list when saving a subset (leading to references to the same list). Real code pattern: curr.append(element); ... ; curr.pop().

examples.trace: Start with empty list []. Perform append(1): list is now [1]. To backtrack, perform pop(): list becomes [] again.
Start with list [1]. Append(2): list becomes [1,2]. Pop(): removes 2, list back to [1].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Recursion and Base Cases
ladders.desc: Grasp the basic idea of recursion: a function calling itself with a smaller problem, stopping at a base case.
ladders.explanation: Recursion is a programming technique where a function calls itself to solve a smaller instance of the same problem. It consists of two parts: the base case and the recursive case. The base case is the simplest scenario that can be solved directly without further recursion, preventing infinite loops. The recursive case breaks the problem down into smaller subproblems, calls the function recursively, and combines results. For subset generation, the recursion processes elements one by one: the base case is when no elements are left to process (index equals array length), at which point we record the current subset. The recursive case handles the current element by including or excluding it and recursing on the remaining elements. The intuition is that recursion naturally mirrors the decision tree of subset choices. Time complexity depends on the number of recursive calls; here, it's O(2^n). Space complexity includes the recursion stack, O(n). Connection to L4: Recursion uses list operations to build subsets. Connection to higher levels: This foundation is essential for implementing the backtracking helper. Common mistakes include missing base case (causing stack overflow), or not reducing problem size in recursive calls. Real code pattern: def recursive_function(params): if base_case_condition: do_something; return; else: recursive_function(updated_params).

examples.input: Recursive function to count down from n to 0
examples.trace: Define count_down(n): if n < 0: return (base case). Else: print(n); count_down(n-1) (recursive case). Example: count_down(2): n=2, not base, print 2, call count_down(1). n=1, not base, print 1, call count_down(0). n=0, not base, print 0, call count_down(-1). n=-1, base case, return. Done.

examples.input: Recursive function to compute factorial of n
examples.trace: Define factorial(n): if n == 0: return 1 (base case). Else: return n * factorial(n-1) (recursive case). Example: factorial(3): n=3, not base, return 3 * factorial(2). Call factorial(2): n=2, not base, return 2 * factorial(1). Call factorial(1): n=1, not base, return 1 * factorial(0). Call factorial(0): n=0, base, return 1. Unwind: 1*1=1, 2*1=2, 3*2=6, return 6.

testCases.input: Recursive tower of Hanoi with 3 disks

approaches.name: Approach 3: Backtracking Iterative Build
approaches.summary: Start with an empty subset and iteratively add elements to build new subsets from existing ones, using a loop within recursion to avoid duplicate work. This approach is efficient for incremental subset construction.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for recursion stack, plus O(n * 2^n) for output

ladders.title: Full Problem — Generate All Subsets Using Backtracking Iterative Build
ladders.desc: Complete solution to generate all subsets of an array using recursion with iterative element addition.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the subset generation problem by starting with an empty subset and systematically building all possible subsets through recursive exploration. The key insight is to treat subset construction as a tree where each level corresponds to including or excluding elements, but here we use an iterative loop within recursion to add elements one by one, avoiding duplicate subsets. The intuition is that at each recursive call, we have a current subset, and we iterate through the remaining elements to create new subsets by adding each element to the current subset and recursing further. This ensures all combinations are explored without repetition. The algorithm begins with an empty subset and the full array. In each recursive step, we add the current subset to the result, then loop through the array starting from a given index. For each element in the loop, we append it to the current subset, make a recursive call with the next index, and then backtrack by removing the element to explore other possibilities. This backtracking is crucial to reuse the same subset list efficiently. The time complexity is O(n * 2^n) because there are 2^n subsets, and each subset takes O(n) time to copy or process. The space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack and O(n * 2^n) for storing all subsets in the output. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete recursive function that uses the iterative build pattern. Common mistakes include not backtracking properly (leading to incorrect subsets), forgetting to add the initial empty subset, or mishandling indices causing duplicates. To avoid these, always remove the added element after recursion and ensure the loop starts from the correct index. Real code pattern: def subsets(nums): result = []; def backtrack(start, current): result.append(current[:]); for i in range(start, len(nums)): current.append(nums[i]); backtrack(i + 1, current); current.pop(); backtrack(0, []); return result.

examples.trace: Start: result = [], current = [], start = 0. Step 1: Add current [] to result → result = [[]]. Loop i from 0 to 1: i=0: Append nums[0]=1 to current → current = [1]. Recurse backtrack(1, [1]): Add [1] to result → result = [[], [1]]. Loop i from 1 to 1: i=1: Append nums[1]=2 to current → current = [1, 2]. Recurse backtrack(2, [1, 2]): Add [1, 2] to result → result = [[], [1], [1, 2]]. Loop i from 2 to 1 (no iteration). Backtrack: pop 2 → current = [1]. Backtrack: pop 1 → current = []. i=1: Append nums[1]=2 to current → current = [2]. Recurse backtrack(2, [2]): Add [2] to result → result = [[], [1], [1, 2], [2]]. Loop i from 2 to 1 (no iteration). Backtrack: pop 2 → current = []. End: return result = [[], [1], [1, 2], [2]].

Start: result = [], current = [], start = 0. Step 1: Add current [] to result → result = [[]]. Loop i from 0 to 0: i=0: Append nums[0]=3 to current → current = [3]. Recurse backtrack(1, [3]): Add [3] to result → result = [[], [3]]. Loop i from 1 to 0 (no iteration). Backtrack: pop 3 → current = []. End: return result = [[], [3]].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Backtracking Function with Iterative Loop
ladders.desc: Implement the core recursive function that builds subsets by iterating through elements and backtracking.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the approach, responsible for generating subsets through recursion and an inner loop. The intuition is to explore all possible subsets by, at each step, taking the current subset and adding each remaining element one by one, then recursing to build further, and backtracking to try other elements. This avoids duplicates by using a start index to ensure we only consider elements that haven't been used yet in this branch. The algorithm defines a function backtrack(start, current) where start is the index to begin iterating from, and current is the list representing the subset being built. First, add a copy of current to the result list. Then, loop i from start to len(nums)-1. In each iteration, append nums[i] to current, call backtrack(i+1, current) to explore subsets including this element, and then pop the last element from current to backtrack and try the next element in the loop. This ensures that after exploring all subsets that include nums[i], we remove it to revert current to its state before adding nums[i], allowing the loop to continue with the next element. The time complexity for this function, when integrated, contributes to O(n * 2^n) due to the recursive calls and loop iterations. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion depth. This connects to L0 by being the main function called to solve the full problem. Common mistakes include modifying current without copying when adding to result (leading to all subsets being the same), incorrect start index causing infinite recursion or missed subsets, and forgetting to pop after recursion. To avoid, always append current[:] to result, use i+1 as the next start to prevent reusing elements, and ensure pop is called. Real code pattern: def backtrack(start, current): result.append(current[:]); for i in range(start, len(nums)): current.append(nums[i]); backtrack(i + 1, current); current.pop().

examples.trace: Call backtrack(0, []): Add [] to result. Loop i=0: Append 1 → current = [1]; call backtrack(1, [1]): Add [1] to result. Loop i=1: Append 2 → current = [1, 2]; call backtrack(2, [1, 2]): Add [1, 2] to result. Loop i=2: no iteration. Pop 2 → current = [1]. Pop 1 → current = []. i=1: Append 2 → current = [2]; call backtrack(2, [2]): Add [2] to result. Loop i=2: no iteration. Pop 2 → current = []. End.
Call backtrack(0, []): Add [] to result. Loop i=0: Append 3 → current = [3]; call backtrack(1, [3]): Add [3] to result. Loop i=1: no iteration. Pop 3 → current = []. End.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterative Element Addition within Recursion
ladders.desc: The technique of using a loop inside recursion to add elements sequentially and avoid duplicate subset generation.
ladders.explanation: This core logic is the innovative part of the approach: combining recursion with an iterative loop to build subsets incrementally. The intuition is that instead of making two recursive calls per element (include/exclude), we use a loop to iterate through available elements and add each to the current subset, then recurse to continue building. This reduces redundant work and naturally orders subsets. The algorithm works by maintaining a start index that indicates where to begin the loop in the array. At each recursive call, we loop from start to the end of the array. For each index i in the loop, we add the element at i to the current subset, make a recursive call with start set to i+1 (to avoid reusing the same element), and after the call, remove the element to backtrack. This ensures that all subsets are generated exactly once because the start index prevents going backwards and creating permutations of the same set. The time complexity arises from the loop running for each recursive call, leading to O(n * 2^n) overall. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. This connects to L1 as the mechanism inside the backtrack function. Common mistakes include setting start to i instead of i+1, causing infinite recursion or duplicate subsets, or not incrementing start correctly. To avoid, always pass i+1 to the recursive call to move forward in the array. Real code pattern: for i in range(start, len(nums)): current.append(nums[i]); backtrack(i + 1, current); current.pop().

examples.trace: At start=0, current=[]. Loop i=0: Add 1 → current=[1]; recurse with start=1. After recursion, pop 1 → current=[]. Loop i=1: Add 2 → current=[2]; recurse with start=2. After recursion, pop 2 → current=[]. End.
At start=1, current=[3]. Loop i=1: Add 4 → current=[3, 4]; recurse with start=2. After recursion, pop 4 → current=[3]. End.

testCases.expected: Loop i=1 adds b, i=2 adds c after backtrack
Loop i=0 adds 5 (if start allows, but typically start=0 for empty)
Loop i=0 adds 1, i=1 adds 1 after backtrack
Loop i=0 adds 3 (but current already has it)

ladders.title: Building Block — Managing Current Subset with Append and Pop
ladders.desc: Learn to maintain a mutable list for the current subset, using append to add elements and pop to backtrack.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the technique of using a single list to represent the current subset being built, and modifying it dynamically with append and pop operations. The intuition is that by reusing the same list, we save space and time compared to creating new lists at each step, but we must carefully manage state changes to avoid errors. The algorithm involves starting with an empty list for the current subset. When we want to include an element, we append it to the list. After exploring all subsets that include that element (via recursion), we pop it from the list to revert to the previous state, allowing us to try the next element. This backtracking is essential to ensure the list reflects the correct subset at each point in the recursion tree. The time complexity for these operations is O(1) per append/pop, but they are called many times in the overall algorithm. Space complexity is O(n) for the list size. This connects to L2 as the way elements are added and removed within the loop. Common mistakes include forgetting to pop after recursion (leading to subsets that accumulate all elements), or popping the wrong element. To avoid, always pair each append with a pop in the same scope, and ensure pop is called after the recursive call returns. Real code pattern: current.append(element); explore(); current.pop().

examples.trace: Start: current = []. Append 1: current = [1]. After some operations (e.g., recursion), pop: remove last element → current = [].
Start: current = [a]. Append b: current = [a, b]. After operations, pop: remove b → current = [a].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through Array with Start Index
ladders.desc: Fundamental loop that goes through array elements from a given start index to the end.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves writing a loop that iterates over an array, but starting from a specified index rather than always from the beginning. The intuition is to control which elements are considered at each step, which is crucial for avoiding duplicate work in subset generation. The algorithm uses a for loop with a variable i that runs from start to len(array)-1. At each iteration, i takes on the value of the current index, and we can access array[i] to get the element. This allows us to process only the elements that haven't been used yet in the current recursive branch. The time complexity is O(n) for a full iteration, but in context, it runs multiple times. Space complexity is O(1) for the loop variable. This connects to L3 as the mechanism that drives the append/pop operations. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors, such as using i <= len(array) or forgetting to handle empty arrays. To avoid, ensure the loop condition is i < len(array) and check if start is within bounds. Real code pattern: for i in range(start, len(array)): element = array[i]; process element.

examples.trace: Loop i from 1 to 2: i=1 → element=2; i=2 → element=3.
Loop i from 0 to 0: i=0 → element=a.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Basic knowledge of arrays, how to access elements by index, and simple iteration.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the fundamental concept of arrays (or lists in Python), which are ordered collections of elements. The intuition is that an array allows us to store multiple items in a sequence, and each item can be accessed using its position, called an index. Indexing starts at 0, so the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. The algorithm for basic array operations includes: creating an array, accessing an element by index (e.g., array[0] gives the first element), and iterating through all elements using a loop. For example, a for loop can go through each element one by one. This is essential for the subset problem because we need to process each element of the input array. The time complexity for accessing an element by index is O(1), and iterating through all elements is O(n). Space complexity is O(n) for storing the array. This connects to L4 as the prerequisite for iterating with a start index. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing (thinking index 1 is the first element) or going out of bounds (accessing index equal to or greater than array length). To avoid, remember indices start at 0 and always check the array length before accessing. Real code pattern: array = [1, 2, 3]; first = array[0]; for element in array: process element.

examples.trace: Array has elements: index 0 = 10, index 1 = 20, index 2 = 30. Access array[0] → 10. Iterate: for element in array: first iteration element=10, second element=20, third element=30.
Array has one element: index 0 = a. Access array[0] → a. Iterate: for element in array: only iteration element=a.
